user_query:
I’ve got a mixed late-summer harvest and want a one-day preservation plan that gives me mostly shelf-stable results with safe, tested-method guidance. Please read these files to understand what I have and my constraints:
- input/pantry.csv
- input/preferences.json
- input/altitude.txt

Deliverables (write these under output/):
1) output/batch_plan.json — a machine-checkable plan with one object per batch, covering what we’ll make, exact ratios, and safety-critical parameters. Include at least: sauerkraut (dry-salted ferment), a tomato product (with added acid for canning), a fruit item (peaches) for water-bath canning, and either cucumbers or green beans preserved in a way that’s shelf-stable and safe. The JSON must include, at minimum:
- altitude_ft (copied from input/altitude.txt)
- batches: [ ... ] where each batch has:
  - name (string)
  - primary_ingredient (string)
  - method (one of exactly: "fermentation_dry_salt", "fermentation_brine", "water_bath_canning", "dehydration", "quick_pickling")
  - jars: [{ size_oz (number), count (integer) }] to show jar allocation from what I have
  - For method == "fermentation_dry_salt": veg_total_weight_g (number), salt_percent (number), salt_g (number), keep_submerged (boolean)
  - For method == "fermentation_brine": brine_percent (number), brine_total_ml (number), keep_submerged (boolean)
  - For method == "water_bath_canning": recipe_type (string like "tomatoes" | "fruit" | "pickles_vinegar" | "salsa"), process_time_base_min (number), process_time_adjusted_min (number), acidity_percent (number, if pickled), is_pickled (boolean), vinegar_ratio (number, vinegar_ml/water_ml), and for tomato products either lemon_tbsp_per_quart (number) or citric_acid_tsp_per_quart (number)
  - For method == "dehydration": category (e.g., "herbs" | "fruit_leather"), either air_dry (boolean) or target_temp_F (number)
  - notes (string) with any tested-recipe reference and safety caveats

Rules you must follow in the plan:
- Never water-bath can low-acid foods unless they’re pickled with at least a 1:1 vinegar:water ratio using 5% acidity vinegar.
- Tomatoes must get added acid: at least 2 tbsp bottled lemon juice or 1/2 tsp citric acid per quart.
- Adjust all water-bath processing times for my altitude using standard increments: +0 min (≤1000 ft), +5 min (1001–3000 ft), +10 min (3001–6000 ft), +15 min (6001–8000 ft), +20 min (8001–10000 ft).
- Dry-salted ferments must be ~2% salt by weight; brine ferments must be 3–5% brine; keep ferments fully submerged.
- Prefer shelf-stable outcomes given my limited fridge space; if you include any quick_pickling or fermentation that requires refrigeration after, keep it minimal and justify why.
- Use the jars listed in input/preferences.json; if I’m short, list the deficit on the shopping list.

2) output/instructions.md — detailed, step-by-step instructions for the full day, with timing, temperatures (F and C), headspace, jar prep, altitude adjustments, yields, labeling, and safety warnings. Explicitly include guidance about botulism prevention, the 2% salt rule, the 3–5% brine range, why to keep ferments submerged, the minimum 5% vinegar requirement, and why altitude matters. Include troubleshooting tips. Keep it realistic for one day based on time_available_hours.

3) output/shopping_list.csv — a concise list of anything I need to buy with columns: item, quantity, unit, purpose (e.g., "new flat lids", "extra 5% vinegar", "citric acid", "non-iodized salt"). Include jar/lid shortfalls if any.

4) output/schedule.txt — a practical timeline for the day (prep, canning runs by batch, cool-downs, ferment setup checks), with a rough total hours estimate that respects the time window in input/preferences.json.

Important: base your plan strictly on food safety best practices. Do not suggest water-bath canning of unpickled low-acid vegetables (e.g., plain green beans). If you choose to make dilly beans or cucumber pickles shelf-stable, ensure vinegar:water is at least 1:1 with 5% vinegar. Please use both metric and US units throughout the instructions, and explain any assumptions you make.
